steps.title: The Oligarch's Shadow

activity.name: Vieux Port de Cannes — Quai Laubeuf Investigation

steps.narrative_intro:
Your phone vibrates twice as you leave the Palais. A new contact has appeared in your app — no name, just an avatar of a chess knight in black. The message is already there, timestamped forty seconds ago:

"Port. Now. Quai Laubeuf. The yacht with the blue tarp — second berth from the fuel dock. 90 seconds of looking and you'll understand why Kask died."

Before you can process this, another message arrives — this one from Lena Voss, Europol liaison, her tone clipped and professional:

"We've detected movement on the Castre Protocol. Someone tried to spoof your wallet signature eleven minutes ago. Failed. They'll try physically next. I need you at the Vieux Port — I'm tracking a vessel connected to Kask's ledger. The seized assets from the Morozov sanctions case are moored there. What you see will confirm why this case is worth killing for. Move quickly — I can't protect digital ghosts."

Two characters. Same destination. Very different reasons for sending you there. The Vieux Port is a three-minute walk south. The masts are already visible between the buildings. Somewhere among those gleaming hulls sits the carcass of a sanctioned oligarch's yacht — and the physical proof that Kask's blockchain forensics weren't paranoid fantasy.
steps.instruction: Walk south to the Vieux Port, Quai Laubeuf side. When you arrive, find the largest or most conspicuous luxury yacht moored along the quay. Photograph its hull, stern, or any identifying details (name, registration port, flag). Then study the surrounding boats and the quay itself — Lena Voss needs you to count the number of superyachts (over ~20 meters) visible from your position. Report the count and your photo to confirm you're on location and observing the right area. This is where seized oligarch assets rot in plain sight — and where Kask traced the first crypto-to-real-estate conversion.

tension.description: Voss and Specter both sent the player to the same location but for different reasons. Voss wants the player to see the scale of the laundering operation to motivate cooperation with Europol. Specter wants the player to notice something specific Voss won't mention — that the yacht's maintenance payments prove the network is still active, not dismantled as Europol claims. The player's count of superyachts becomes a data point: if they report accurately, Specter will later use that number to reveal which other vessels are connected to the same shell company.
tension.resolution: The player's photo and count are received by both Voss and Specter (who intercepts). Each reacts differently — Voss praises diligence, Specter sends a cryptic one-line response referencing a specific detail in the photo the player may not have consciously noticed. The contradiction between their reactions is the first crack in the narrative.

character_interactions.phone_version: Voss responds within moments, clinical and precise: 'Good eye. That's consistent with the Morozov seizure profile — 73 meters, Maltese flag of convenience, built by Lürssen in 2009. It's been sitting there since March 2022. Four years of rot at €340K a month in maintenance. Someone's still paying. Kask traced those payments to the same wallet cluster that converted the Carlton stones. This isn't ancient history — it's an active pipeline. How many large yachts can you count from your position? I need to cross-reference against our asset registry. Every discrepancy is a lead.'

rayban_version.script: Voss's voice is steady, professional, with an undercurrent of controlled urgency: 'That matches the Morozov seizure profile. Seventy-three meters, Maltese flag, Lürssen build. It's been rotting there since 2022 — but someone still pays three hundred forty thousand a month to keep it afloat. Kask traced those payments to the same wallets that converted the Carlton jewels. I need you to count every superyacht visible from your position. Every single one over twenty meters. Discrepancies between what you see and what's in our registry will tell me which boats are ghosts — registered under shells we haven't cracked yet. Take your time. Get it right.'

camera_mode.purpose: AI analyzes yacht photo for identifying features — name, flag, hull details — and Voss reacts to specifics

character_interactions.trigger: Player reports yacht count to Voss
character_interactions.phone_version: A single message from the chess knight avatar, no greeting, no context: 'You counted [X]. Three of those aren't in any registry. Kask knew which ones. Now you need to know why Castaldi does too.' Then silence. The message auto-deletes after 30 seconds, leaving only a screenshot prompt.

rayban_version.script: A distorted voice, barely above a whisper, cuts through your audio for exactly eight seconds: 'Three of the yachts you just counted don't exist in any registry. Kask knew which ones. Find out why Castaldi does too.' The audio cuts to silence.

character_interactions.phone_version: A new contact appears — Enzo Castaldi, profile photo showing a tanned man in expensive sunglasses, mid-laugh. His message is warm, conspiratorial, immediately familiar: 'So you're Kask's chosen one. I was wondering when you'd show up at the port. Don't let Voss turn you into an evidence mule — she's been circling these yachts for two years and has nothing. I actually KNEW Soren. Drinks later? I know a place on Rue Meynadier that serves a rosé he loved. First though — what did you see down there? Genuinely curious. The port tells different stories depending on who's looking.' His tone is the opposite of Voss's sterile professionalism — it's the warmth of someone who wants to be your friend. Dangerously likable.

rayban_version.script: A rich baritone, the kind of voice that orders for the table without asking: 'Ah, so you're the one Soren picked. Welcome to the circus. Listen — ignore whatever Voss just told you. She's been counting yachts for two years and she's no closer to anything. I actually knew Kask. Liked him. He was paranoid, but he was right. I can help you understand what you're looking at down there, but I need to know — what did you see? What caught your eye? The port tells very different stories depending on who's listening.' A pause, then softer: 'I'll be in touch. Don't freeze me out — you'll regret it.'

verification.target: Luxury yacht at Quai Laubeuf + superyacht count
verification.success_condition: Player sends a clear photo of a yacht showing identifiable features (name, flag, hull details, or general profile) AND provides a numerical count of superyachts visible from their position
verification.success_reaction: Voss grants elevated access: 'Your count matches our satellite data within one. You're observant — that's rare. Uploading Kask's port analysis to your vault. He mapped every vessel here to a wallet address. You're going to need this.' A detailed document appears showing Kask's yacht-to-wallet mapping for the Cannes Vieux Port. +30 XP investigation bonus. Specter's next message arrives faster and with more detail than it would otherwise.
verification.failure_fallback: If the photo is unclear or the count is missing, Voss adapts: 'I can work with partial data. The important thing is you're there, you've seen the scale of this. I'm sending you Kask's port analysis anyway — you'll need it for what comes next. But next time, precision matters. These people don't make mistakes, and neither can we.' The document still appears in the vault but with additional redactions. Specter's message is delayed and terser.
verification.timeout_reaction: After 6 minutes, Voss messages: 'We're burning daylight — literally. The Protocol auto-destructs at 16:00 and I still need you moving. If you couldn't get a clear shot, fine. But I need you headed to your next position. Sending coordinates now. The port told you what it needed to — money doesn't just disappear, it changes shape. Remember that.'

steps.player_action: Photograph the largest luxury yacht visible at Quai Laubeuf (hull, stern name, or identifying features) and count the total number of superyachts (20m+) visible from your position along the port. Report both the photo and count to Voss.

gps_trigger.content_brief: A declassified Europol asset seizure report (partially redacted) appears in the vault. It details the 2022 seizure of the 73m yacht 'Seraphim' belonging to Viktor Morozov under EU sanctions regulation 269/2014. Key detail: the yacht's maintenance costs of €340,000/month are being paid by an anonymous shell company registered in Malta — the same jurisdiction where Kask traced three of the Carlton jewel-to-crypto conversion wallets. A handwritten annotation in the margin reads: 'Follow the maintenance money — it's the same pipeline. —S.K.' confirming Kask had access to this document before his death.

steps.camera_prompt: The player photographs a luxury yacht in the Vieux Port of Cannes. AI should identify any visible yacht name, flag, registration details, and the general scale/opulence of the vessel. Narratively, the AI interprets the yacht as connected to the Morozov sanctions case — the character Voss will react to specific details visible in the photo (hull color, any visible crests or logos, the state of maintenance or neglect) and tie them to the money laundering narrative. If a tarp or covering is visible, Specter will note it as evidence of attempted concealment. Any real yacht names or ports of registration should be woven into the fiction as shell company fronts.
steps.blockchain_event: Castre Protocol status update: validator proximity confirmed at Vieux Port coordinates. Second of six geolocation proofs logged to the contract. Network alert triggered — an unknown wallet attempted to register a competing validator address 4 minutes ago. Attempt rejected. Clock reads 14:23. Time remaining: 1h 37m.
steps.unlock_message: VAULT UPDATED — Europol Asset Seizure Report #EU-2022-MOROZOV-0041 declassified. Kask's margin annotations visible. The maintenance payments on the seized yacht trace to the same Maltese shell cluster that converted the Carlton jewels. The pipeline is still flowing. Someone is still feeding it.

steps.title: The Carlton's Memory

activity.name: Carlton Hotel Facade Investigation — La Croisette

steps.narrative_intro:
Maro's voice is still in your head — the tremor when she said her father's name, the steel underneath it. She sent you a fragment of Kask's ledger: a column of Ethereum wallet addresses, each one tagged with a date and a property sale in Cannes. The first entry is dated July 28, 2013 — the same day armed men walked into the Carlton InterContinental and walked out with €103 million in jewels. That's not a coincidence. That's a genesis block.

The Carlton sits on La Croisette like a gilded jaw. You've seen it in photographs — the Belle Époque cupolas, the white facade, the kind of architecture that makes theft feel theatrical. But Kask didn't care about theatre. He cared about what happened AFTER. According to his partial ledger, within 72 hours of the heist, three shell companies were registered in Cyprus. Within six months, those companies purchased apartments visible from where you're about to stand. The jewels became crypto became concrete. And right now, Enzo Castaldi — the man who knows every stone on this boulevard — is offering to walk you through exactly which buildings. He's been texting you since the port. Charming. Knowledgeable. Eager.

Specter just sent a single line: 'Count the cupolas. Not the ones you see.'

You have no idea what that means. Yet.
steps.instruction: Walk east along La Croisette from the Vieux Port toward the Carlton Hotel (3-4 minutes). When you arrive at the Carlton facade, photograph the building's distinctive twin cupolas from across the boulevard. Then: both you AND another player in the quest must independently count the number of balcony levels visible on the central facade between the two cupolas and report the same number to Maro via the app. Kask embedded a two-validator consensus check — if two independent observers confirm the same architectural detail, it unlocks the next ledger fragment. This is blockchain logic applied to the real world. Maro is waiting.

tension.description: Castaldi floods the player with useful-seeming architectural intel about the Carlton and surrounding buildings, naming real streets and dates, building trust rapidly. Meanwhile Specter's cryptic cupola message and the vault document hint that Castaldi's knowledge is suspiciously precise — how does a 'local historian' know the exact renovation timeline of a hotel cupola linked to money laundering? The collaborative consensus requirement forces the player to commit to an observation, creating the first irreversible data point in the ledger reconstruction.
tension.resolution: Whether the player's count matches or not, Maro unlocks a partial ledger fragment. If it matches, the fragment is complete and reveals the redacted address — which turns out to be a building Castaldi personally brokered the sale of in 2017. If it fails, the fragment is partial but Maro manually overrides with a comment: 'Close enough. My father taught me that consensus is a negotiation, not a religion.'

verification.target: Number of distinct balcony levels on the Carlton's central facade between the twin cupolas
verification.success_condition: Player's count matches the second validator's independent count (the real answer is 7 distinct balcony levels on the central facade)
verification.success_reaction: Full ledger fragment 3 unlocks in the vault, revealing the redacted Croisette property address — which is a building Castaldi brokered. Maro's trust increases significantly. Specter sends a rare second message: 'You see clearly. That matters more than you know.' +15 XP bonus.
verification.failure_fallback: Maro manually overrides the consensus with Kask's backup key, unlocking a partial fragment missing one property address. She texts: 'Consensus is a negotiation. We keep moving. But you're flying with one eye closed now — that missing address will matter at the end.' Story continues with slightly less information available for the final validation decision.
verification.timeout_reaction: After 6 minutes, Castaldi texts: 'Still admiring the view? I don't blame you. But time is a factor today, remember? Send Maro your count — any count. Kask's system accepts approximations if you're honest about your confidence level. That's the difference between blockchain and bureaucracy.'

steps.player_action: Photograph the Carlton Hotel's twin cupolas and central facade from across Boulevard de la Croisette. Count the exact number of distinct balcony levels visible between the two cupolas on the main facade. Report this number through the app. Your count must match another player's independent observation to unlock the next ledger fragment — Kask designed a real-world consensus mechanism.

gps_trigger.content_brief: A partial page from Kask's forensic ledger auto-unlocks in the vault. It shows three Ethereum wallet addresses tagged 'CARLTON-GENESIS' with timestamps from July 2013, each linked to a Cyprus shell company name and a Cannes property address on or near La Croisette. One address is redacted — the validator fragment needed to reveal it is locked behind the consensus check. A handwritten annotation in Kask's writing reads: 'The cupolas are a lie. One was rebuilt in 2009. Ask yourself who paid for the renovation and why their name appears on line 7.'

steps.camera_prompt: The player photographs the Carlton InterContinental Hotel's iconic facade, focusing on the twin Belle Époque cupolas and the balconied central section. AI vision identifies the cupolas, balcony levels, architectural details, and any renovation differences between the two domes. The AI character (Castaldi) uses real architectural features to weave in narrative about which apartments visible from this vantage point were purchased with laundered crypto-converted jewel proceeds, making the real Croisette skyline feel like a crime scene map.
steps.blockchain_event: FRAGMENT_3_CONSENSUS — Ledger fragment 3 (Volkov-Croisette property chain) unlocked via meatspace consensus protocol. Two independent validators confirmed matching architectural observation. Smart contract state: 3/6 fragments recovered. Castre Protocol auto-destruct timer: 2h47m remaining.
steps.unlock_message: CONSENSUS PROTOCOL ACTIVE — Kask's ledger requires two independent validators to confirm the same real-world observation. Your count has been submitted. Waiting for second confirmation. Fragment 3 of the Castre Protocol is assembling.

steps.title: The Laundry Architecture

activity.name: MIPIM Real Estate Corridor Investigation — Rue d'Antibes

steps.narrative_intro: Three blocks behind the Croisette's golden facade lies Cannes' real circulatory system — Rue d'Antibes, where luxury real estate agencies cluster like barnacles on a hull. Every March during MIPIM, this street becomes a corridor of handshakes worth nine figures. Kask's ledger mapped twelve properties purchased between 2016 and 2023 using layered shell companies — all managed through agencies on this street. The jewels became Bitcoin. The Bitcoin became Ethereum. The Ethereum became seafront penthouses. You're standing in the washing machine. Castaldi just sent you an address and a name — the agency that brokered the largest transaction. He says it'll crack the ledger wide open. But Specter's last message was three words: 'Castaldi brokers too.' You have forty-seven minutes until the Protocol burns. The contradictions are piling up faster than the evidence.
steps.instruction: Walk from La Croisette up to Rue d'Antibes (3 minutes). Find a luxury real estate agency storefront — the kind that displays seafront properties with seven-figure price tags in the window. Photograph the property listings displayed in the agency window. Castaldi needs you to find a specific detail: any listing that references the neighborhoods of Le Suquet or La Californie, the two zones where Kask's ledger flagged laundered purchases. Count how many listings in the window exceed €2 million and report this to Maro, who is cross-referencing against Kask's transaction map. Then describe what you see in the window to Nadia — she'll tell you something nobody else will.

tension.description: The Specter document that auto-unlocks on arrival directly contradicts Castaldi's story — Adriatic Horizon SCI, which Castaldi mentioned as a legitimate client, appears on Kask's ledger as a laundering vehicle. The player now holds proof that their most helpful ally has been steering them. Simultaneously, Maro pushes the player to focus only on properties that could exonerate her father, while Voss demands the full picture. The player's photo of real listings becomes a Rorschach test — each character interprets it differently based on their agenda.
tension.resolution: The player decides how much of what they've found to share with whom. Sharing the Adriatic Horizon connection with Castaldi tests whether he deflects or confesses. Sharing the full count with Maro reveals whether she suppresses data points. Sharing everything with Nadia triggers her first moment of genuine honesty about the irreversible consequences of validation.

character_interactions.trigger: Player sends photo of real estate agency window
character_interactions.phone_version: Castaldi responds with infectious enthusiasm: 'Magnificent work. See the listings in the €3-5M range? That's the sweet spot — high enough to move serious volume, low enough to avoid the Tracfin triggers. Kask mapped every single one.' He then casually asks the player to focus on a specific agency name, steering them AWAY from Adriatic Horizon SCI. If the player mentions Adriatic Horizon (from Specter's document), there's a two-second pause before Castaldi smoothly replies: 'Ah. You've been reading ahead. Yes, I consulted for them — years ago, purely architectural assessments. Kask misread the connection. It happens when you're paranoid enough to build a dead man's switch.' His charm doesn't waver, but the explanation is one beat too rehearsed.

rayban_version.script: Castaldi's voice is warm, rapid, seductive — the sound of someone who's never been caught off-guard. He narrates the real estate window like a sommelier describing a wine list, identifying price brackets and neighborhoods with unsettling expertise. If the player mentions Adriatic Horizon, his rhythm breaks for exactly one breath before recovering. 'You've been reading ahead. Impressive. And dangerous.'

camera_mode.purpose: AI analyzes the real estate window display and Castaldi references specific visible listings by price or location, weaving them into the laundering narrative with disturbing specificity

character_interactions.trigger: Player reports the count of listings over €2M
character_interactions.phone_version: Maro's response is terse, urgent: 'How many above two million? Good. Now listen — my father was a jeweler in Split before all this. The Panthers used his shop as a front without his knowledge. Three of the properties on Kask's ledger were purchased to frame him as a beneficiary. I need you to tell me: are any of those listings in Le Suquet? Because those are the ones that prove his innocence. The others... the others can wait.' She's asking you to prioritize HER truth over the complete picture. If the player pushes back, she snaps: 'Voss doesn't care about collateral damage. She'll burn my father to catch bigger fish. Is that justice?' If the player complies, she softens: 'Thank you. You're the first person in three years who listened.'

rayban_version.script: Maro speaks fast, clipped, a woman running out of time and patience. Her accent thickens when she's emotional. She asks specifically about Le Suquet listings. If the player hesitates, her voice drops: 'I'm not asking you to lie. I'm asking you to look in the right direction first.' The desperation is real. The manipulation might be too.

character_interactions.trigger: Player describes the agency window or shares any findings with Nadia
character_interactions.phone_version: Nadia responds differently from everyone else — she doesn't react to the listings at all. Instead: 'Before you go further, I need to tell you something no one else will. When Kask built the Castre Protocol, the validation doesn't just preserve the ledger. It triggers automatic filings with the Tribunal de Grande Instance in Paris, the Estonian Financial Intelligence Unit, and Europol's SIENA network. Your wallet address — YOUR address — becomes part of the permanent evidentiary chain. You will be subpoenaable. In every jurisdiction. Voss knows this. Castaldi definitely knows this. I'm telling you because in thirty-nine minutes you're going to make a decision, and you deserve to make it with open eyes.' A pause. 'I liked Soren. He was brilliant and paranoid and kind. But he chose you without asking. That's not fair. So I'm asking: do you still want to do this?'

rayban_version.script: Nadia's voice is steady, almost clinical, but cracks once on the word 'kind' when describing Kask. She lays out the legal consequences of validation with the precision of someone who's read the smart contract line by line. No manipulation, no urgency games — just the truth, delivered like a surgeon describing a procedure. 'Your wallet becomes evidence. Permanently. In every jurisdiction Kask flagged. I'm telling you because nobody else will.'

verification.target: Luxury real estate agency window display on Rue d'Antibes with visible property listings
verification.success_condition: Photo shows a real estate agency storefront with visible property listings, prices, or neighborhood references. AI can identify agency name, price ranges, or location details.
verification.success_reaction: Castaldi sends a follow-up: 'You have a forensic eye. Kask would've liked you.' +15 XP. Maro unlocks a supplementary document in the vault: a side-by-side comparison of three shell company purchase dates matched to MIPIM conference dates 2017-2022, proving the laundering was seasonal and systematic. The player's trust with Nadia increases — she sends a brief addendum: 'You're still here. That tells me something about you.'
verification.failure_fallback: If the player can't find or photograph a suitable agency window, Maro sends a screenshot from Google Street View of Rue d'Antibes agencies: 'Can't stop. Use this. The third agency from the left — that's the one Kask flagged. Keep moving.' The story continues with the same revelations, delivered through Maro's impatience rather than the player's discovery. Nadia's warning about validation consequences still arrives on schedule.
verification.timeout_reaction: After 6 minutes, Voss breaks radio silence with a clipped message: 'We're monitoring the Protocol timer. Thirty-two minutes. Whatever you're doing on Rue d'Antibes, do it faster or move on. The next location matters more than this one.' Castaldi follows fifteen seconds later, more casually: 'She's right, for once. The real prize is ahead. Don't get lost in the shop windows.'

steps.player_action: Photograph a luxury real estate agency window display on Rue d'Antibes showing high-end property listings. Count the number of listings priced above €2 million. Look for any properties in Le Suquet or La Californie neighborhoods specifically. Send the photo and your count to the characters through the app.

gps_trigger.content_brief: A partial page from Kask's ledger appears in the vault — a transaction map showing twelve property purchases between 2016-2023, each linked to a shell company. Three addresses are on Rue d'Antibes. One shell company name is highlighted in red: 'Adriatic Horizon SCI' — the same name Castaldi casually mentioned in Step 2 as 'a client.' The document is timestamped with Kask's digital signature, dated two days before his death.

steps.camera_prompt: The player photographs a real estate agency window display on Rue d'Antibes. AI identifies the agency name, visible property listings, price ranges, neighborhood names, and architectural style of displayed properties. The AI character weaves these real details into the money-laundering narrative — connecting specific price ranges or neighborhoods to Kask's ledger entries. If Le Suquet or La Californie properties are visible, this is treated as a direct hit on the laundering map.
steps.blockchain_event: Kask's smart contract emits a 'VALIDATOR_PROXIMATE' event as the player's GPS coordinates align with one of the twelve flagged property addresses on Rue d'Antibes. The player's wallet receives a micro-transaction of 0.00001 ETH with the memo: 'Node 7/12 confirmed. Spatial proximity validated. Castre Protocol integrity: 74%. Time remaining: recalculating.' This is the first indication that Kask's system is tracking the player's physical movements against the ledger's geographic data.
steps.unlock_message: A new file appears in your vault: KASK_LEDGER_PARTIAL_07.pdf — twelve transactions, twelve properties, twelve shell companies. One name glows like a lit fuse: Adriatic Horizon SCI. You've seen that name before. Castaldi mentioned it. He said it was nothing. Kask thought it was everything. One of them is lying. In thirty-nine minutes, it won't matter — unless you validate.

steps.title: The Notary's Ledger

activity.name: Marché Forville Investigation — Cross-Referencing the Castre Protocol
activity.address: Marché Forville, 6 Rue du Marché Forville, 06400 Cannes

steps.narrative_intro: Rue d'Antibes gave you the shape of the laundering network — shell companies nesting inside luxury storefronts like parasites in marble. But shapes aren't proof. Proof is what Soren Kask built, and proof is what's disintegrating inside the Castre Protocol's countdown timer. You have fragments now — transaction hashes from the Vieux Port, property records from the Croisette corridor, the Carlton connection. But fragments don't validate. Kask's smart contract requires a specific confirmation sequence, and the final decryption seed is encoded in a pattern he called 'the notary's signature' — a cryptographic hash derived from the architectural geometry of Cannes' oldest commercial structure. The Marché Forville. Built in 1934, rebuilt after the war, its iron-and-stone arcade has served as the backbone of Cannes commerce for nearly a century. Kask visited it seventeen times in his last three months alive. He photographed it obsessively. He told Nadia it was 'the only honest building left in this city.' Now you're about to find out why he died staring at pictures of a fish market.
steps.instruction: Make your way to the Marché Forville — the covered market just two blocks north of Rue d'Antibes. When you arrive, Nadia Ferhat will send you a decrypted fragment of Kask's research journal. Your task: photograph the main stone archway entrance of the market, counting the number of iron support columns visible along the facade. This number is the geometric seed Kask used for the final decryption layer. Then cross-reference what Castaldi and Voss have told you about the timeline — one of them has lied about when the crypto conversion happened, and the contradiction is hidden in a property registration document Nadia will unlock. Report your column count AND which character you believe is lying to Nadia. Your answer determines which validation path the Protocol follows.

tension.description: The player must actively cross-reference contradictory information from Castaldi and Voss to determine who lied about the crypto conversion timeline. The Kask journal fragment reveals that BOTH characters have hidden something — Castaldi lied about the date, but Voss deliberately buried evidence that would have exposed the network earlier. Accusing Castaldi is factually correct but lets Voss off the hook. Accusing Voss is factually incomplete but reveals a deeper institutional betrayal. The player's choice shapes which version of the truth the validated ledger will tell.
tension.resolution: Nadia receives the player's accusation and column count. She reveals that Kask designed the Protocol to accept EITHER answer — because both are partially true. But the validation path differs: accusing Castaldi triggers a full public ledger (exposing everyone, including Voss's suppression), while accusing Voss triggers a sealed judicial submission (protecting the Europol investigation but letting Castaldi's network restructure). Neither path is clean. The player has just made an irreversible choice about what kind of justice they believe in.

character_interactions.trigger: GPS arrival at Marché Forville unlocks Kask's journal page; Nadia messages 90 seconds later
character_interactions.phone_version: Message from Nadia: 'You've seen the journal page by now. I need to tell you something I should have said at the start. I was Kask's legal counsel. Not just his contact — his lawyer. Attorney-client privilege died with him, so I'm free to speak, but that doesn't mean I'm free of guilt. I knew what he was building for eight months before he died. I told him to go to Europol. He did. Voss took his preliminary findings and sat on them for eleven weeks. ELEVEN. In that time, three properties changed hands and two witnesses relocated to Dubai. Kask never trusted institutions again after that. That's why he built the Protocol — a dead man's switch that doesn't need permission from anyone. Except you. Count the structural columns on the Forville facade. Not the decorative pilasters — the load-bearing ones. Kask was specific. And then tell me: who lied to you about the conversion date? I already know the answer. I need to know if YOU do.'

rayban_version.script: Nadia's voice is steady but carries the weight of someone confessing. 'You've seen his journal. I was Kask's lawyer. I should have told you sooner. He went to Europol — to Voss — eight months before he died. She sat on his evidence for eleven weeks. Three properties changed hands. Two witnesses vanished to Dubai. That's why the Protocol exists. It doesn't ask permission. It only asks you. Count the load-bearing columns on the Forville facade. Not decoration — structure. Then tell me who lied about the conversion timeline. I already know. I need to know if you do.'

camera_mode.purpose: Player photographs the Marché Forville facade; AI counts columns and interprets structural vs decorative elements through Kask's cryptographic lens

character_interactions.trigger: Player has been at location for 4 minutes OR opens the Kask journal document
character_interactions.phone_version: Message from Castaldi: 'I see you've reached the market. Lovely place — the bouillabaisse ingredients are extraordinary if you know which stall to trust. Listen, I'm hearing chatter that Nadia Ferhat has been feeding you Kask's personal documents. Be very careful with those. Kask was brilliant but paranoid. He saw conspiracies in architecture — literally. He once told me the column spacing at Forville encoded a Fibonacci sequence that proved money laundering. The man was unraveling. I respected his mind, but his methods became... unreliable. Toward the end, he accused everyone. Me, Voss, the mayor's office, a sommelier at the Majestic. If Nadia is asking you to choose who lied, consider that Kask's own framework may be the lie. The 2018 date I gave you came from verified exchange records. Whatever document Nadia is showing you, ask yourself: was it verified by anyone other than a dead man and his grieving lawyer? I'm trying to protect you from becoming a pawn in someone else's revenge fantasy. Validate the Protocol clean. Don't let Nadia poison the well.'

rayban_version.script: Castaldi's voice is warm, almost paternal, but there's an edge now — the first crack in the charm. 'Reached the market, have you? Beautiful place. Listen — carefully now. Nadia is showing you Kask's journal. Kask was brilliant but he was breaking apart. He saw codes in column spacing, conspiracies in architecture. He accused everyone — me, Voss, a sommelier. The 2018 date I gave you is from verified exchange records. Whatever Nadia has comes from a dead man's paranoia and his lawyer's grief. Don't become a pawn in someone's revenge. Validate clean.'

character_interactions.trigger: Player sends their photo of the Forville facade through the app
character_interactions.phone_version: Message from Specter — no text, just an image: a close-up photograph of the same Marché Forville entrance, taken at night, months ago. In the image, a figure's shadow falls across the columns. The metadata reads: 'October 2025, 02:47 AM.' A single line of text below: 'He counted them seventeen times. He always got the same number. Trust the structure.'

rayban_version.script: A brief audio artifact plays — the sound of footsteps on stone, then silence, then Specter's distorted voice, barely above a whisper: 'He counted seventeen times. Same number every time. Trust the structure. Not the voice that tells you the structure is wrong.'

verification.target: Photo of Marché Forville facade showing structural columns + text message to Nadia identifying which character (Castaldi or Voss) lied about the crypto conversion timeline
verification.success_condition: Player submits a clear photo of the Forville entrance with visible columns AND provides a reasoned accusation naming either Castaldi or Voss, referencing specific contradictions from the Kask journal or previous character statements
verification.success_reaction: Nadia responds with visible emotion: 'You cross-referenced. You actually cross-referenced. Kask said the validator would be thorough — I didn't believe him. Your column count matches his seed value. The Protocol's penultimate layer is unlocked. And your choice about who lied... it shapes the validation path. I won't tell you which path is 'better.' There is no better. There's only what you can live with. One more step. The final confirmation site. Kask chose it for a reason I'm only now beginning to understand.' Player receives +150 XP, trust++ with Nadia, and a classified Europol internal memo (AI-generated) showing Voss's eleven-week delay annotated with her handwritten justification: 'Premature disclosure risks the larger network. Let them move. We track.' The memo is damning either way — it's either strategic patience or criminal negligence.
verification.failure_fallback: If the player's photo is unclear or they don't provide an accusation, Nadia sends: 'Time is compressing. I can work with what you've given me — the Protocol has redundancy Kask built for exactly this situation. The seed defaults to his backup value. We lose granularity in the ledger but not the core evidence. Your accusation... if you can't decide, the Protocol decides for you. It defaults to full exposure. Everyone's sins, on the public chain. Kask's dead man's switch was never subtle.' The story continues — the player moves to Step 6 with the default full-exposure validation path, losing the nuance of choosing but gaining the narrative weight of inaction having consequences.
verification.timeout_reaction: After 8 minutes, Nadia messages: 'The countdown doesn't care about thoroughness. I need your count and your call — Castaldi or Voss. If I don't hear from you in two minutes, the Protocol's backup seed activates and you lose the ability to choose the validation path. Kask wanted you to choose. Don't let the clock choose for you.'

steps.player_action: Photograph the main entrance archway of Marché Forville, count the visible iron support columns along the facade, then cross-reference the contradictory timelines given by Castaldi and Voss to determine which character lied about the crypto conversion date. Report both the column count and your accusation to Nadia.

gps_trigger.content_brief: A decrypted page from Soren Kask's research journal — handwritten notes with architectural sketches of Marché Forville's facade, column measurements, and a margin annotation reading: 'The seed is in the structure. Count what holds the building up — not what decorates it. They'll try to make you count the wrong things. Castaldi already has.' Below: a scanned property registration from 2017 showing a MIPIM-linked shell company purchasing a building on Rue Félix Faure — timestamped March 14, 2017. This contradicts Castaldi's claim that conversions didn't begin until 2018, but aligns with Voss's timeline. However, a second notation in Kask's hand reads: 'Voss knows about Félix Faure. She buried it in her own report. Ask yourself why.'

steps.camera_prompt: The player photographs the stone and iron facade of Marché Forville, focusing on the entrance archway and structural columns. AI identifies the architectural elements — the post-war reconstruction ironwork, the stone arcade, the number of load-bearing columns versus decorative elements. The AI character (Nadia) uses the real architectural details to narratively confirm or challenge the player's count, weaving the actual structure into Kask's cryptographic methodology: 'Kask didn't use decoration. He used load-bearing truth. The columns that hold weight are the ones that matter — just like the evidence.'
steps.blockchain_event: Castre Protocol penultimate decryption layer unlocked — player's column count serves as geometric seed for the final validation key. The validation path (public ledger vs. sealed judicial) is now locked based on the player's accusation. A micro-transaction of 0.00001 ETH is sent to the player's wallet with memo: 'SEED CONFIRMED — FINAL VALIDATION PENDING — FORVILLE/[column_count]'
steps.unlock_message: A page from a dead man's journal materializes in your vault. The handwriting is meticulous, almost architectural. Soren Kask was here before you. He counted what holds this building up. Now it's your turn to count — and to decide whose lies matter more.

steps.title: The Validator's Mask

activity.name: Notre-Dame d'Espérance — The Validation Point
activity.address: 1 Place du Suquet, 06400 Cannes (Église Notre-Dame d'Espérance, Le Suquet)

steps.narrative_intro:
The climb to Le Suquet feels like ascending out of the city's reach. Below you, Cannes spreads its postcard lie — the white yachts, the golden facades, the Croisette shimmering like a promise nobody intends to keep. Up here, the old town doesn't care about any of it. The medieval stone walls of Notre-Dame d'Espérance have watched empires launder themselves clean for six centuries.

Your phone buzzes with a contract notification. The Castre Protocol is now in its final validation window. The smart contract's countdown timer reads 00:47:12. Less than an hour. Every piece you've gathered — Kask's transaction map, the yacht seizure records, the Carlton conversion trail, the MIPIM property matrix, the cross-referenced ledger from Forville — feeds into a single cryptographic confirmation that only your wallet can execute.

But something Nadia said won't leave you alone: validation is irreversible. And something Specter sent won't stop flashing behind your eyes: that single word — LOOK.

You are standing at the highest point in Cannes. You can see everything from here. The question is whether you've seen enough to know what you're about to do.
steps.instruction: Climb to the esplanade of Notre-Dame d'Espérance at the top of Le Suquet. Stand where you can see both the Vieux Port and the Palais des Festivals below. The Castre Protocol requires a final validator confirmation — but before you execute it, you must photograph the panoramic view from Le Suquet looking down toward the sea. This photo becomes your "validator's witness image" — cryptographically timestamped proof that a human being stood at these coordinates and chose to act. Then you must make your choice: tell Nadia whether you validate the FULL ledger, validate SELECTIVELY (Maro's version), or let the protocol burn. Every character will react to your decision in real time.

tension.description: The player must make an irreversible choice with incomplete information. FULL validation triggers automatic international court filings that will expose the network but also permanently place the player on record as a key witness in an international financial crimes case. SELECTIVE validation protects Maro's father but lets parts of the network escape. BURNING the protocol destroys Kask's life work but keeps the player safe. Castaldi is flooding messages urging speed and full validation — because he believes a rushed, unexamined full validation will be easier for his legal team to challenge as coerced. Maro is begging for selective. Voss is demanding full through official channels. Only Nadia tells the player what validation actually triggers. Only Specter — now revealed as Kask himself — stays silent after his final audio.
tension.resolution: There is no clean resolution. Each choice has consequences that ripple differently. The orchestrator builds the ending from the player's choice, their accumulated trust levels with each character, and which contradictions they caught along the way. The twist recontextualizes everything: Castaldi WANTED full validation because his legal team prepared a challenge. The 'best' outcome requires the player to have listened to Nadia's warning about irreversibility AND caught Castaldi's lies AND still chosen to validate fully despite knowing the personal risk — because that's what Kask's audio asked for: a person who decided.

character_interactions.trigger: GPS arrival at Le Suquet esplanade
character_interactions.phone_version: Nadia sends a long message — her longest of the entire quest. No flirtation, no double meanings. Raw: 'I need you to understand something before you touch that contract. I should have told you earlier. I didn't because I was afraid you'd walk away, and I needed you not to walk away. Validation doesn't just preserve the ledger. It triggers automatic filings with the International Criminal Court, Eurojust, and the French Parquet National Financier. Your wallet address — YOUR address — becomes part of the evidentiary chain. Permanently. You will be called to testify. Your name will appear in sealed documents that won't stay sealed forever. Castaldi knows this. Voss knows this. Maro doesn't care. I'm the only one telling you because... because Soren would have told you. He was honest like that. Annoyingly, irreversibly honest. Whatever you choose — I'll still be here after. That's not nothing.' She then asks directly: 'What's your decision? Full, selective, or burn?'

rayban_version.script: Nadia's voice comes through the glasses — intimate, close, as if she's standing right beside the player on the esplanade. She delivers her confession about validation triggering automatic court filings. Her voice breaks slightly on 'I should have told you earlier.' She pauses. Wind sound fills the gap. Then, quieter: 'He was honest like that. Annoyingly, irreversibly honest.' Another pause. 'Whatever you choose. I'll still be here after. That's not nothing.' Then she asks for the player's decision.

camera_mode.purpose: Player photographs the panoramic view as their validator witness image while Nadia speaks — the AI identifies the landmarks and maps them to every node of the conspiracy investigated today

character_interactions.phone_version: Castaldi sends a rapid burst of messages, charming and urgent: 'Darling, ignore the dramatics. Nadia loves a speech. Listen — full validation, no hesitation. This is what Soren wanted. This is what justice looks like. You've done the work, you've seen the evidence, now CONFIRM it before the clock runs out. Don't let a dead man's failsafe destroy a dead man's masterpiece. You're better than that. I always knew you were.' Then, a beat later: 'And between us — the selective option is Maro trying to protect her war-criminal father. You didn't come this far to help a Pink Panther's daughter rewrite history, did you?' His tone is perfectly calibrated — warm, conspiratorial, flattering. Every word is designed to prevent the player from thinking too hard about WHY he wants full validation so urgently.

rayban_version.script: Castaldi's voice through the glasses — smooth, warm, with the faintest edge of urgency he can't quite hide. He urges full validation, dismisses Nadia's warning as dramatics, subtly attacks Maro's motives. His charm is at maximum. But if the player has been paying attention, they'll catch it: he never once addresses Nadia's revelation about the court filings. He talks around it. That silence is the loudest thing he's said all day.

character_interactions.phone_version: Maro's message arrives with a different energy than before — no bravado, no provocative jokes. Quiet: 'I know what I asked you. Selective validation. Protect my father's name. I still want that. But I heard Kask's audio too — don't ask me how. He said he chose you because you were clean. Maybe clean means you don't let someone like me edit the truth. Maybe it means you do what's right even when right is ugly.' A pause. Then: 'My father did what he did. I've spent ten years pretending the jewels just vanished into nothing. They didn't. They became apartments and shell companies and men like Castaldi. If full validation takes my father down with the rest of them... at least it's real. I won't hate you. I might not answer your calls for a while. But I won't hate you.' She doesn't explicitly change her request — she gives the player permission without saying so.

rayban_version.script: Maro's voice through the glasses — stripped of her usual sharp humor. She speaks slowly, as if discovering what she thinks in real time. She acknowledges her father's guilt obliquely. She gives the player quiet permission to choose full validation without explicitly withdrawing her earlier request. Her voice is steady but thin. She ends with: 'I won't hate you. I might not answer your calls for a while.' The line goes silent.

verification.target: Player's validation decision and reasoning sent to Nadia
verification.success_condition: Player sends a clear decision (FULL, SELECTIVE, or BURN) with at least one sentence of reasoning that demonstrates engagement with the narrative tensions — referencing information gathered during the quest, character contradictions they noticed, or the personal risk Nadia revealed
verification.success_reaction: Nadia responds with genuine emotion. If FULL: 'You just put yourself on a permanent record. Soren would have liked you. I already do. The filings are transmitting. Check your vault — there's something from Kask. A final document he left for whoever validated.' The vault unlocks Kask's original research notebook (AI-generated PDF) — a personal, annotated timeline of his three-year investigation, with a handwritten note on the last page: 'To the validator — thank you for finishing what I started. The mask is off.' If the player caught Castaldi's lies during the quest (cross-referencing contradictions), Voss sends a follow-up: 'We flagged Castaldi's communications. Your hesitation at the right moments told us everything. He's been detained at Nice airport. Well played.' +150 XP bonus. The blockchain event mints the Validator's Mask NFT with the player's witness photo embedded.
verification.failure_fallback: If the player chooses SELECTIVE: Nadia accepts it quietly. 'I understand. Some truths are too heavy to carry whole.' The ledger is partially preserved. Maro sends a single heart emoji. Castaldi goes suspiciously silent — because selective validation actually serves him too, removing the most damaging evidence. Voss sends a cold message: 'Partial evidence is worse than none in court. But it's your name on the contract, not mine.' The quest ends with an ambiguous tone — justice half-served, the network wounded but alive. The player still receives the Validator's Mask NFT but it's marked 'PARTIAL' on-chain. If BURN: The protocol auto-destructs. Kask's work disappears. Specter sends one final message — just a period. Nothing else. Nadia: 'I told you I'd still be here. I am. But it's quieter now.' Castaldi sends champagne emojis, then deletes them. The player receives no NFT — but Maro sends them Kask's personal photo, a man smiling on a Helsinki dock, and says: 'He looked like this. Someone should remember.' The quest ends in silence.
verification.timeout_reaction: At 3 minutes without a decision, Nadia sends: 'The clock doesn't care about doubt. 00:12:33 remaining. I need your answer.' At 6 minutes, Specter breaks silence with a single text: 'Decide.' At 8 minutes, the protocol countdown becomes visible in the app — a real-time timer. At 10 minutes, Nadia sends: 'I'm executing full validation on your behalf in 60 seconds unless you tell me otherwise. Soren didn't choose you so you could freeze.' This forces a response or defaults to full validation — the story always completes.

steps.player_action: Photograph the panoramic view from the Le Suquet esplanade showing the Vieux Port and Palais des Festivals below — this becomes your cryptographic witness image. Then send your validation decision to Nadia: FULL (complete ledger), SELECTIVE (Maro's redacted version), or BURN (let it auto-destruct). State your choice clearly and explain WHY in at least one sentence.

gps_trigger.content_brief: A 22-second audio file unlocks — the first and only time Specter uses a human voice instead of text. It is Soren Kask's voice, recorded before his death. He speaks with a soft Finnish accent, exhausted but calm: 'If you're hearing this, you climbed the hill. Good. I chose you because your wallet was clean — no DeFi yield farms, no mixer transactions, no rug pulls. Just a person. That's what the ledger needs. Not an institution. Not a cause. A person who looked at the evidence and decided. Whatever you decide, it was yours. That's the only thing they can't launder.' The audio ends. No music. No follow-up. Just silence and the wind at Le Suquet.

steps.camera_prompt: The player photographs the panoramic view from Le Suquet looking down toward the Mediterranean, showing the Vieux Port with its yachts, the Palais des Festivals, and the Croisette stretching east. The AI interprets this as the validator's witness frame — noting the real landmarks visible (Tour du Suquet, Port, Palais roofline, Île Sainte-Marguerite in the distance) and weaving them into the narrative as the geographic coordinates of every node in Kask's ledger: the port where the oligarch's yacht was seized, the Palais where ETH Global hosted the last conference Kask attended, the Croisette where the Carlton heist occurred, the real estate corridor where MIPIM deals laundered the jewels into property. The entire conspiracy is visible from this single vantage point. The AI tells the player: everything they investigated today is contained in this one frame.
steps.blockchain_event: CASTRE_PROTOCOL_VALIDATED — Player's decision (FULL/SELECTIVE/BURN) is recorded on-chain. If FULL or SELECTIVE: the Validator's Mask NFT is minted to the player's wallet, embedding their witness photo from Le Suquet, a hash of all evidence gathered during the quest, and their decision timestamp. The NFT metadata includes: validator_address, witness_image_hash, decision_type, evidence_nodes_confirmed (count of verifications passed during quest), and kask_memorial_hash. If FULL with Castaldi contradictions caught: a bonus 'Unmasked' trait is added to the NFT. The original 0.0001 ETH from Kask's wallet is symbolically 'returned' via a smart contract callback — completing the loop.
steps.unlock_message: CASTRE PROTOCOL — FINAL VALIDATION WINDOW OPEN. Your wallet is the only authorized validator. The smart contract is listening. The countdown is real. Everything you gathered today feeds into this moment. Kask chose you. Now choose.

narrative_beats.description: THE HOOK TIGHTENS — Noor sends the player a cropped surveillance photo of someone following them near the Croisette. She warns: 'You're already on someone's list. That means you matter — or you're disposable. Let's find out which.' This establishes the danger and makes the player feel watched from the start.
possible_triggers: Player completes first photo verification at Step 1
Player lingers too long at Step 1 location (timeout nudge)

narrative_beats.description: CASTALDI'S TEST — Enzo Castaldi contacts the player unsolicited, claiming he knows why Noor recruited them. He offers a 'gift': a document in the vault showing a 2019 auction lot with provenance that contradicts what Noor told them. He's testing whether the player will share this with Noor or keep it secret. This is the first loyalty fork.
possible_triggers: Player sends photo verification at Step 2
Player mentions Castaldi's name to Noor
Triggered automatically midway through Step 2

narrative_beats.description: SABLE BREAKS SILENCE — The Ghost character Sable sends an unprompted message: a single image of the Carlton's original 1913 blueprints with one room circled in red, plus two words: 'Wrong floor.' This contradicts information both Noor and Castaldi have given. The player must decide who to share this with — or keep it for themselves.
possible_triggers: Player has interacted with both Noor and Castaldi by this point
Player photographs a specific architectural detail that Sable's AI finds relevant
Timed trigger 90 seconds after arriving at Step 3

narrative_beats.description: MILO'S CONFESSION — Milo Vuković, increasingly nervous, sends a voice message admitting he forged provenance documents for three pieces in the 2013 Carlton heist recovery. He says Castaldi paid him. But he also says Noor knew — and chose to look away. The player now has leverage over multiple characters. Milo begs: 'Don't tell Sylvie. She'll bury me.' This is the emotional midpoint.
possible_triggers: Player successfully completes the skill verification at Step 3 or 4
Player directly asks Milo about the forgeries
Milo panics after Castaldi mentions him

narrative_beats.description: THE COMMANDANT'S ULTIMATUM — Sylvie Maret calls the player directly (voice call via app). She's clipped, professional, furious. She knows someone has been leaking case files. She gives the player a choice: become her informant on Noor and Castaldi, or she pulls the plug on their access. She reveals she has GPS logs of everywhere the player has been today. 'You're not a tourist anymore. You're a witness — or a suspect.'
possible_triggers: Player arrives at Step 4 or Step 5 location
Player shares Milo's confession with any character
Timed trigger if player has been in contact with 3+ characters

narrative_beats.description: NOOR'S VULNERABILITY — If trust with Noor is medium or higher, she sends a personal message late in the quest revealing why she's really in Cannes: her younger sister was the unnamed 'associate' who disappeared after the 2013 heist. She's not investigating for justice — she's looking for proof her sister is alive. 'I've used you. I know. But everything I've told you about the jewels is true. The rest... the rest is mine.' This recontextualizes her entire agenda.
possible_triggers: Trust with Noor is medium or high
Player has not betrayed Noor to Sylvie
Player asks Noor a personal question

narrative_beats.description: THE TWIST DETONATES — The player discovers (through document cross-referencing, character revelations, or Sable's final drop) that the 2013 Carlton heist jewels were never actually stolen. They were moved by the hotel's own insurance consortium as part of a massive fraud scheme. The 'heist' was staged. Castaldi brokered the insurance. Noor's sister didn't disappear — she was silenced because she found out. The 'Hands of the Carlton' aren't the thieves' hands — they're the hotel's own hands, moving its own jewels.
possible_triggers: Player arrives at final step
Player has assembled 3+ contradictory documents in vault
Sable sends final message
Player confronts Castaldi with Milo's confession

narrative_beats.description: ARG BLEEDTHROUGH — A character (determined by trust state) sends a message to the player's real email or SMS (with prior consent). It's a fake Interpol 'notice of interest' PDF with the player's name on it, referencing the Carlton case. The boundary between game and reality dissolves. Sylvie sends it if she's the dominant relationship; Sable sends it as a coordinates-only email if the Ghost path is strongest.
possible_triggers: Player gave email/phone consent at quest start
Player has high engagement score (completed most verifications)
Triggered between Steps 5 and 6

narrative_tensions: Voss vs. Maro: Europol wants the complete ledger validated and entered into evidence through official channels; Maro wants selective validation that exonerates her father while still exposing the network. These goals are incompatible. The player must navigate between two women who both have legitimate grievances and illegitimate methods.

Castaldi's seduction vs. Specter's warnings: The most charming, helpful, knowledgeable character (Castaldi) is the most dangerous, while the most cryptic, opaque character (Specter) is the most trustworthy. The player must resist the narrative instinct to trust warmth over silence.

Time pressure vs. thoroughness: The 16:00 auto-destruct creates urgency, but rushing means missing contradictions between characters that reveal who's lying. Speed favors Castaldi (less scrutiny); deliberation favors truth (but risks the ledger).

Validation as irreversible action: Nadia's hidden knowledge that validation triggers automatic international court filings means the player's final action has consequences beyond what anyone has told them. Do they validate knowing it's irreversible? Do they validate without knowing?

The dead man's agency: Kask (as Specter) is still pulling strings from the shadows. The player is simultaneously a free agent and a piece in a dead man's chess game. How much of their 'choice' is real?

Institutional trust vs. individual justice: Voss represents a system (Europol) that failed to protect Kask and may be compromised. Maro represents personal justice that could undermine the larger case. Neither path is clean.

The player's own exposure: By validating, the player puts themselves on the permanent record of an international financial crimes case. Every character downplays this risk for their own reasons. Only Nadia is honest about it.

twist.description: Soren Kask is not dead. He is Specter — the Ghost who has been sending the player cryptic messages throughout the quest. He faked his death when he discovered a mole inside Europol (someone in Voss's unit, not Voss herself) was feeding information to Castaldi. The entire Castre Protocol — the validator system, the timed auto-destruct, the selection of THIS specific player — was Kask's elaborate failsafe designed from his position as a 'dead man' who could no longer be surveilled or silenced. Every character the player has been talking to has been, in some way, a piece that Kask positioned on the board before his disappearance. The museum at Le Suquet — the Musée des Explorations du Monde — is the final validation point because Kask embedded the confirmation key in the museum's own digital acquisition registry (lot 247, a Mediterranean nautical instrument acquired in 2021 through a Castaldi shell company). The player doesn't just validate a ledger — they expose that a piece of the museum's own collection is stolen cultural property purchased with laundered diamond money. The dead man was alive all along, and the validator was never random — the player's wallet was chosen because it was created on the exact block that contains a hash of Kask's daughter's birthdate. Kask isn't protecting evidence. He's protecting his family's future.
revelation_variants: PATH A — HIGH SPECTER TRUST: If the player has consistently followed Specter's guidance and asked 'Are you Kask?' at least once, Specter sends a final message at the museum: 'lot 247. look at the acquisition date. now look at the block height of your wallet creation. same number. not random. never random. — soren.' The player realizes Specter IS Kask and that they were chosen deliberately.

PATH B — HIGH VOSS TRUST: If the player has earned Voss's full trust, she shares the unredacted Europol file at the museum which shows Kask's death certificate was never officially filed with Finnish authorities — only with French police. She realizes in real-time, messaging the player: 'The death certificate... it was only filed locally. Never with Helsinki. That's not protocol. Unless — unless he filed it himself.' The player and Voss discover the truth together.

PATH C — HIGH MARO TRUST: If the player has sided with Maro, she reveals at the museum that her father received a coded message two days ago from 'a dead Finnish man' offering to testify on his behalf — if the ledger was validated completely, not selectively. She realizes Kask is alive and has been using the player to force HER hand: validate everything or lose her father's one chance at exoneration.

PATH D — HIGH CASTALDI ENGAGEMENT: If the player has shared significant information with Castaldi, he becomes increasingly agitated at the museum step, sending messages that reveal he KNOWS Kask might be alive — 'I saw the body. I SAW it. Someone is playing a very dangerous game.' His panic exposes his involvement (he shouldn't have seen the body if he was just a concerned citizen) and confirms Kask is alive by proving someone powerful enough went to verify the death personally.

PATH E — NADIA REVEAL: If the player has high trust with Nadia, she breaks down at the final step: 'I knew. I'm sorry. He made me promise. He said if I told anyone he was alive, they'd find him and finish it for real. The perfume — I kept smelling his cologne in the code comments. He's still writing. He's still alive.'

resolution_principles: If trust with Noor is HIGH and trust with Castaldi is LOW → Noor reveals the twist herself in a raw, emotional voice message. She names Castaldi as the insurance broker and begs the player to send the proof to Sylvie. The ending centers on justice for Noor's sister.

If trust with Castaldi is HIGH and trust with Noor is LOW → Castaldi reveals the twist as a boast, framing it as 'the most elegant crime ever committed.' He offers the player a cut of the ongoing scheme. The ending forces a moral choice: complicity or exposure.

If trust with Sylvie is HIGH → she becomes the player's primary ally in the finale. She already suspected the insurance fraud but lacked proof. The player becomes her key witness. The ending has a procedural, justice-served tone — but Sylvie warns: 'The people behind this don't forget names.'

If trust with Milo is HIGH and the player protected his secret → Milo sends the original forged provenance documents as proof, sacrificing himself. His final message: 'I was never brave enough to do this alone. Don't let them say I was nothing.' The ending is bittersweet — justice comes at a human cost.

If the player betrayed Noor to Sylvie AND cooperated with Castaldi → Castaldi realizes the player is useful but dangerous. He reveals the twist as a warning: 'You think you chose a side? There are no sides. There's only the money and the people it buries.' The player is left with the knowledge but no allies.

If trust with Sable is HIGH (player responded to all Ghost messages correctly) → Sable reveals themselves as Noor's missing sister, alive and operating from hiding. The twist lands with maximum emotional impact. Sable's final message is more than two sentences for the first and only time.

If trust is LOW with ALL characters → the twist is revealed through a cold Sable data dump into the vault — documents, photos, wire transfers — with no emotional framing. The player gets the truth but no one to share it with. Characters go silent. The ending is lonely and paranoid.

If the player shared information freely between all characters (high transparency) → multiple characters converge on the truth simultaneously, creating a chaotic finale where Noor, Castaldi, and Sylvie are all messaging at once, each trying to control the narrative. The player must choose whose version of events to validate.

Noor Hadid.low: Becomes transactional and cold. Withholds context — gives instructions without explaining why. Starts feeding the player half-truths to keep them useful but controlled. References the player dismissively: 'Just do what I asked.' Will actively misdirect if she suspects the player is working with Castaldi.
Noor Hadid.medium: Cooperates genuinely but guards her personal stake. Shares real intelligence about the case but never mentions her sister. Tone is warm but measured — professional flirtation, intellectual respect. Drops hints: 'This isn't just a job for me.' Tests the player with small trust exercises before escalating.
Noor Hadid.high: Confides everything — her sister, her guilt, her fear that the truth will destroy what's left of her family. Becomes emotionally raw in voice messages. Trusts the player with the most dangerous piece of evidence. Will sacrifice her own cover to protect the player if Castaldi threatens them. Says things like: 'You're the only person in this city I believe.'

Enzo Castaldi.low: Treats the player as entertainment — a pawn in a game he's already won. Sends mocking messages. Gives deliberately misleading 'gifts' of information. Will attempt to turn other characters against the player. 'You had potential. Past tense.'
Enzo Castaldi.medium: Engages as an equal adversary. Shares real information but always with a hook — every revelation comes with an implied debt. Charm is at maximum. Drops genuine clues mixed with self-serving lies. Treats the exchange like a chess game he's enjoying.
Enzo Castaldi.high: Respects the player as a worthy opponent. Reveals the mechanics of the insurance fraud because he's proud of it. Becomes oddly honest — not moral, but transparent about his amorality. Offers genuine warnings about danger. 'I like you. That's why I'm telling you to stop.'

Sable.low: Goes completely silent. No messages, no coordinates, no images. The Ghost vanishes. If the player ignored or mishandled Sable's earlier messages, they get nothing from this character for the rest of the quest. Absence IS the punishment.
Sable.medium: Continues cryptic drops — images, coordinates, single-sentence messages — but with slightly more context. A timestamp here, a name there. Still no explanation, but the breadcrumbs become followable. The player feels guided by something that might care.
Sable.high: Messages become urgent, almost human. Still minimal, but the tone shifts from cryptic to protective. Sends warnings before dangerous moments. Final revelation (identity as Noor's sister) only unlocks at this level. The last message breaks all established patterns — full sentences, emotion, a name.

Milo Vuković.low: Panics and lies badly. Claims he never forged anything, then contradicts himself two messages later. Tries to cut contact. If cornered, threatens to tell Castaldi that the player is investigating him. Desperate, sloppy, pathetic — but not harmless.
Milo Vuković.medium: Nervous but cooperative. Admits to the forgeries in vague terms ('I may have... adjusted some details'). Provides useful technical information about provenance and authentication but downplays his own role. Seeks reassurance constantly: 'You won't tell anyone, right?'
Milo Vuković.high: Becomes the quest's moral conscience. Fully confesses, provides documentary proof, accepts consequences. Finds courage he didn't know he had. His voice messages shift from shaky to steady. Actively helps the player build the case, even knowing it will destroy him. 'I'm tired of being afraid of my own signature.'

Commandant Sylvie Maret.low: Treats the player as a suspect, not an asset. Issues thinly veiled threats about obstruction charges. Monitors without sharing. Every interaction feels like an interrogation. Will use information the player gave other characters against them — she has sources everywhere.
Commandant Sylvie Maret.medium: Professional respect without warmth. Shares limited case information in exchange for the player's findings. Transactional but fair. Warns the player about Castaldi's danger level — genuinely, not manipulatively. 'I don't trust you. But I trust the others less.'
Commandant Sylvie Maret.high: Becomes a fierce protector. Reveals she's been investigating the insurance fraud angle for two years but was blocked by political pressure. Shares classified documents. Treats the player as her unofficial partner. Her stern exterior cracks — she's exhausted and this case haunts her. 'If we get this wrong, they'll bury it for another decade.'

nft_metadata.city: Cannes — starting point: Palais des Festivals, final destination: Musée des Explorations du Monde (Le Suquet)
